content:
5 min read
Just now

--

Your system didn’t get worse. It got bigger. Those aren’t the same problem but they need the same fix.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Your architecture isn’t broken. It’s just too small for what you built on top of it.
Every architecture is right until it isn’t.
The monolith that let you ship fast at 3 engineers is the same monolith that now takes a war room to deploy at 30. The database schema that made sense for one product line is now bent into six unrelated shapes. Nobody made a bad decision. The decision just expired.
The hard part is that architecture doesn’t fail loudly. It doesn’t throw an exception. It shows up as a slow, creeping tax , a standup that runs ten minutes longer than it used to, a deploy that everyone quietly dreads, a “quick fix” that takes three days because nobody can find the blast radius anymore.
Here are five signs that tax has become due.
1. Nobody can explain the system in one sitting
Ask a new hire to explain what the codebase does , not the file structure, the actual behavior and watch how long the explanation takes.
In a healthy architecture, someone can sketch the core flow on a whiteboard in ten minutes: here’s how a request comes in, here’s what touches the database, here’s where the money moves. When that sketch turns into a twenty-minute tour through six services, three shared libraries, and “oh, and then there’s this other thing that also writes to that table,” you’ve lost something important: a shared mental model.
This matters more than it sounds like it should. Teams don’t just ship code against a codebase, they ship code against their understanding of a codebase. Once that understanding fragments, every change becomes an archaeology project before it becomes an engineering task.
2. Every change requires touching five files that “shouldn’t” be related
You want to add a field to an order. That should mean: update the model, update the API, done. Instead it means updating the order model, the analytics pipeline, the notification service, the admin dashboard, and a caching layer that nobody remembers writing.
This is the smell of concerns that were never truly separated, just placed in different files and called modular. The architecture looks decomposed on a diagram, but the actual coupling lives in shared assumptions, implicit contracts, and data that multiple systems reach into directly instead of asking for.
A useful gut check: when you make a small change, does the size of the pull request match the size of the feature? If a one-sentence feature request turns into an eight-file diff every time, the architecture is fighting you, not helping you.
3. Your test suite is either too slow to trust or too shallow to matter
Architecture debt shows up in testing in two opposite-looking ways, and both mean the same thing.
Either the suite has grown so entangled with the system that a single unit test spins up half the application to run, turning CI into a coffee break or the team gave up on that and tests got shallow, mocking so much that they verify nothing except “the mocks work.” Either way, confidence has quietly detached from the tests passing.
You’ll usually find the real symptom next to it: engineers start manually testing in staging before every release, “just to be safe.” That instinct is correct and it’s also a vote of no confidence in the architecture, cast with their feet.
4. Onboarding takes weeks longer than the code complexity should justify
Complexity is expected to cost onboarding time. What’s not expected and what’s a real signal that’s when onboarding time is dominated by tribal knowledge instead of code complexity.
If new engineers spend their first month learning “oh, don’t touch that service on Fridays” and “that config actually lives in three places, only one of which matters” and “yeah the docs are wrong, ask Sarah” , the codebase isn’t complex, it’s undocumented-by-design. The architecture has outgrown the point where it can be understood from the code alone, and it’s now running on institutional memory that leaves the company every time someone quits.
5. Scaling the team doesn’t scale your output
This is the one leadership actually feels.
You double the engineering team expecting roughly double the throughput, and instead you get 20% more output and twice the merge conflicts. More people are now stepping on each other in the same shared modules, waiting on the same brittle deploy pipeline, and debugging the same tangled dependency graph.
This is the clearest tell of all, because it’s not a code-quality problem — it’s a boundary problem. Good architecture is, among other things, a tool for letting teams work independently. When adding people slows you down instead of speeding you up, the system’s boundaries no longer match your org’s boundaries, and no amount of individual skill will fix that from the inside.

What to actually do about it
None of this means “rewrite everything”, that’s how you get the sequel to this article, the one about the rewrite that killed the team. Architecture debt is paid down the same way technical debt is: incrementally, and only where it’s actually blocking you.
A few honest next steps, roughly in order of how disruptive they are:

• Draw the system as it actually is, not as the onboarding doc claims. The gap between those two diagrams is your problem list.

• Find your most-changed files (git log will tell you) and check whether they're touched often because they're central, or because they're a dumping ground. Those are different problems with different fixes.

• Pick one boundary, not all of them. Carve out the single most painful coupling — the one causing the most cross-team friction — and fix that first. Prove the pattern works before you scale it.

• Make the fix visible in velocity, not just in diagrams. If a boundary change doesn’t measurably reduce PR size, deploy fear, or onboarding time within a quarter, you probably drew the boundary in the wrong place.
Architecture isn’t something you get right once. It’s something you keep re-fitting to a team and a product that won’t stop changing shape. The codebases that age well aren’t the ones that avoided debt, they’re the ones where someone noticed the signs early enough to still have options.
